g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 3 on Ricardian Contracts - bridging legal agreements with blockchain technology.
Smart contracts execute code automatically, but they lack legal context - courts need human-readable proof of agreement.
g.text: Smart contracts are just executable code (if-then logic)
They lack legal intent and aren't evidence in court

g.data-narration: A Ricardian Contract is a digital document that is both human-readable and machine-readable, cryptographically signed for legal enforceability.
g.text: A hybrid digital document combining legal prose with structured data,
proposed by Ian Grigg (1990s) - named after economist David Ricardo.
digital format, bridge between law and code."

g.data-narration: Four pillars make Ricardian contracts unique: human-readable, machine-readable, cryptographically signed, and blockchain-linked.
The five-step process: Drafting, Hashing, Signing, Linking to blockchain, then Execution with reference to terms.
The document is hashed using SHA-256, creating a unique digital fingerprint - any change produces a completely different hash.
g.text: ⚠ Tamper Proof: Changing even one character produces a completely different hash!

g.data-narration: Parties cryptographically sign the hash with their private keys - this provides non-repudiation and proves agreement.
g.text: ✅ Non-repudiation: Cannot deny signing this specific document

g.data-narration: The cryptographic hash is stored on the blockchain, creating an immutable anchor between legal text and on-chain execution.
g.text: 🔗 Any future dispute: compare stored hash with current document hash

g.data-narration: Real example: In DeFi lending, users sign a Ricardian contract stating loan terms before the smart contract executes the loan.
g.text: "Lender agrees to provide 2000 DAI to Borrower...
Liquidation if collateral < 125% of loan value"
🔑 User signs Ricardian Contract hash BEFORE transaction → Hash stored in smart contract

g.data-narration: Four key advantages: legal enforceability, clarity, non-repudiation through signatures, and tamper-proof storage.
Three main challenges: technical complexity in drafting, uncertain legal jurisdiction, and limited industry adoption.

g.text: Difficult to create legally sound AND machine-parsable documents
Legal enforceability depends on local laws recognizing digital contracts
Many projects focus only on code, ignoring legal framework

g.data-narration: The key distinction: Ricardian contracts are the lawyer defining terms, smart contracts are the clerk executing logic - they're complementary.
g.text: ⚖️ THE LAWYER - Defines terms & legal intent

g.data-narration: Summary: Primary goal is legal enforceability, core innovation is cryptographic linking, key component is the hash, role is legal framework.
Real applications include DeFi loans, supply chain agreements, and digital securities - anywhere legal enforceability matters.
In summary, Ricardian contracts add crucial legal clarity to blockchain, making DeFi and real-world applications more robust and trustworthy.
g.text: Ricardian Contracts are NOT replacements for Smart Contracts - they are ESSENTIAL complements.
→ They add legal clarity, enable dispute resolution, and make blockchain ready for real-world business.
